modules.name: Supply Chain Analytics I: Introduction to Supply Chain Analytics
modules.description: Module description: In this module, I will give you an introduction to supply chain analytics, share my journey, and preview the talent crisis. By real-life examples, you will also learn how supply chain management (SCM), aided by analytics, may create a long-term competitive advantage. You will see why analytics is critical to SCM. Finally, you will identify the main domains of a supply chain. Module outline:
modules.matched_lecture_name: Supply Chain Analytics I: Introduction to Supply Chain Analytics

lectures.lecture_name: Supply Chain Analytics I: Introduction to Supply Chain Analytics

modules.name: Supply Chain Analytics II: Supply chain pain points and how analytics may relieve them
modules.description: Through real-life examples, you will learn how analytics can be applied to various domains, such as sourcing, production, logistics, and selling, of a supply chain to generate economic values. Module outline:
modules.matched_lecture_name: Supply Chain Analytics II: Supply chain pain points and how analytics may relieve them

lectures.lecture_name: Supply Chain Analytics II: Supply chain pain points and how analytics may relieve them

modules.name: Supply Chain Analytics III: Supply chain analytics (SCA) job opportunities and preparation
modules.description: Module description: In this module, you will learn supply chain analytics job opportunities, requirements, and preparation. You will also make data-driven career decisions using a powerful, easy-to-use, and free database with built-in visualization and analytics tools. Module outline:
modules.matched_lecture_name: Supply Chain Analytics III: Supply chain analytics (SCA) job opportunities and preparation

lectures.lecture_name: Supply Chain Analytics III: Supply chain analytics (SCA) job opportunities and preparation

modules.name: Supply Chain Analytics IV: Inventory Analytics
modules.description: Inventory analytics is the cornerstone of supply chain analytics. Through real-life examples and data, you will learn the data analytics skills to assess the importance of inventory in your company and industry, quantify the impact of inventory on financial performance, and discover inventory problems. You will also learn how to classify inventory and manage them accordingly. Module outline:
modules.matched_lecture_name: Supply Chain Analytics IV: Inventory Analytics

lectures.lecture_name: Supply Chain Analytics IV: Inventory Analytics

modules.name: Supply Chain Analytics V: Sourcing Analytics
modules.description: Sourcing is becoming increasingly data-driven because (1) the global supply market is vast and ever-changing with many potential suppliers, (2) a typical company sources hundreds of millions of dollars of goods from thousands of suppliers. Data analytics is critical to getting more value with less spend. Through the real-life example of Apple's iPhone, you will learn how to apply data analytics and business intelligence to supplier selection and negotiation. Module outline:
modules.matched_lecture_name: Supply Chain Analytics V: Sourcing Analytics

lectures.lecture_name: Supply Chain Analytics V: Sourcing Analytics

modules.name: Introduction to Metaverse
modules.description: Module description: Is metaverse the latest fad or the future of our globally connected community? No-one knows for sure, but we know Meta has bet over $100B in R&D on it so far. This module offers an introduction to the metaverse to serve as foundation for more advanced topics such as new product development, Blockchain, AI, and cryptocurrencies. It is designed to be thought-provoking and can be combined with other exploratory modules in creating a 1 credit BYOC course. Module Outline

modules.name: Intro to Audit Automation
modules.description: Automation is a technology by which a process or procedure is executed with minimum human assistance. The application of automation is increasing its popularity in both accounting and auditing. This module introduces the basic concepts of automation, with emphasis on Robotic Process Automation (RPA), comparisons between traditional automation and RPA, the combination of RPA and AI, and the current state of RPA.

modules.name: Deep learning hands-on session: Building Neural Network with Google Colab
modules.description: This lecture delves into the realm of neural networks, exploring their intricacies and demonstrate its applications. Beginning with an introduction to artificial neurons, the presentation shifts focus to the history, advancements, and contemporary tools, such as PyTorch, that make neural network development accessible. Emphasis is laid on understanding foundational concepts, like hyperplanes, the Universal function approximation theorem, and the formation of feed-forward networks. Then, the practical hands-on session showcases training an autoencoder neural network using a dataset of journal entries to detect anomalies. By leveraging Google Drive, GPU acceleration, and the PyTorch platform, the lecture demonstrates the process of training, model convergence, and the application of neural networks in auditing by identifying irregularities in financial transactions.
modules.matched_lecture_name: Deep learning hands-on session: Building Neural Network with Google Colab

modules.name: Transformational Supply Chain Leadership 5: Two-Pronged Supply Chain Leadership
modules.description: As the world, and especially supply chains become more digital and automated, the connectivity across people becomes of elevated importance. Supply Chain leaders who want to transform their organization, best practices, and business goals must remain “human” in the design and execution of their supply chain. Building and bolstering relationships founded on trust is a core quality all transformational leaders in supply chain deploy
modules.matched_lecture_name: Transformational Supply Chain Leadership 5: Two-Pronged Supply Chain Leadership

that's a d when you think
transformation,
that's dangerous because then you're
doing the same thing you've done forever
and that's sort of the anti of
transformation. So as a leader, what you
have to do is you can't fall into that
sort of rigor of same problem, same
functions, same perspectives. You need
to broaden. So I definitely like that
that thought there.
All right. Um, I do want to make a
portion of this conversation
very tangible to the students, right?
So, for you as somebody who's, you know,
10 years out of school at this point,
I'm right behind you at that same
number. Um, what would be
recommendations you would give to these
students as they aspire to be leaders?
They can be leaders in supply chain or
just leaders in general. What would be a
skill or or a quality or set of
qualities that you would definitely um
make sure that they had if you could
say, "Hey, John, 10 years ago, I wish
you did this so that now I could be
harvesting that." What would be skills
or qualifications that you would
definitely recommend that they pursue?
>> So, being agile as a leader is the most
important trait you can have. Everyone
on your team is going to have different
strengths. They're going to have
different weaknesses. And learning how
to manage each one of those people is
crucial for your success as a leader and
for your team. Being rigid will really
just hold not just yourself back, but
the entirety of your team. If you're
forcing team members into specific boxes
that isn't a fit for their skill set,
you're not going to get the most
accurate and efficient work from them.
Learning how your team works will allow
everyone to contribute at their max
capacity and it will be best for the
organization. Um,
if you're not able and you know we we
kind of talked about this in the
previous question like being flexible is
key. Everyone is going to learn at a
different rate. You might have one
person you get up to speed in six
months. You might have another person
you get up to speed in a year. You can't
hold everyone to a perfect timeline or
perfect expectations.
You have to learn each individual in
your team and allow them to flex to
their strengths. And that's how your
team as a whole can get their greatest
success.
>> Yeah. And I and I think a lot of the
times those expectations
um are not expected. Believe it or not,
I think in in lower level leadership
roles sometimes it's almost accepted
that the person can be sort of rigid and
they have a specific way of working and
they have a specific way of interacting
with people. But what I've seen is the
further you progress in your career, the
more uh challenging that becomes because
if you're not changing, then as the
people around you change, as the
business direction changes, as the
strategy changes, you're sort of stuck
on the same rock that you've been on for
so long. So, I would definitely say
agility, like you said, is is definitely
one of those skills. And and how do you
practice that? Again, it's working with
it's in your class projects, it's in
your internships, listening, seeing
other people's styles. The best way you
can learn anything, whether you're
talking about sports, whether you're
talking about trading stocks, is looking
at what others are doing, right? Noting
down what's working, wording, writing
down what's not working. And everybody
on that's viewing this video right now
has a different leadership style. Nobody
leads the exact same way. That's why
there's some people that are president
of the United States and some people
that are not because different
leadership styles yield different
results. So I also want to celebrate to
everybody on here is don't think as
though John and I are telling you
there's a specific way to lead. Right?
What we're giving you today is
perspectives. Think about agility should
be a component of your leadership style.
Now how agile? That's dependent on you.
That's dependent on your organization.
Right? How open to perspectives? again
dependent on you, dependent on your
organization. So I want to I want to as
we reach the close out here just really
the takeaway messages everything that
you've heard in the session is is a
perspective and something to learn from.
It's a tip. It's a it's a nugget so that
when you're crafting your leadership
style because believe it or not you have
to craft a leadership style. Um you can
take individual segments to say okay
when I'm building Mike as a leader this
is what he looks like. So, um, I just
wanted to sort of point that out here as
we get close to wrapping up that it's we
don't have the answer. John and I do not
have the answer, Kate, but we do and we
have seen that some tips and tricks that
have worked in the past um that we
definitely want to highlight uh through
the conversation today.
All right. Um, I did have one other
question because it's I asked the other
speaker on on my behalf the same
question. really led to just a really
good discussion is to me and again my
perspective a leader is made up of two
broad components. A leader is a uh
technical so they can read financial
statements. They understand demand
forecasting. They understand the retail
industry. So there's a technical
component to leadership. But then to me
there's a human aspect. How do you
interact with people? Do you treat
people the right way? How are your
ethics and morals right? Do you
celebrate people with diversity? Do you
think, John, there's what do you think
the balance is between that? Um, do you
think it's a is a 50/50? Hey, it's just
as important it is to have human skills,
it's just as important to have
technical. I'll give you my answer. My
answer is the classical. It depends. But
I'd like to hear your thoughts on sort
of those two components of a leader,
human and sort of the technical business
aspect.
>> Yeah, I I agree with you 100%. There's
so many factors that go into it. It
really does depend. But leadership is
more than just being smart. It's more
than being an expert in your field. It's
more than having the best skills.
Leadership is also about people. You
know, some of the smartest people in
this world are not great managers and
they're not great leaders just because
they don't know how to maximize the
potential of the people on their team or
the people who they manage. So, it's uh
you really have to learn to master the
blend. There's no perfect answer. We
will never be able to create a perfect
leader in a lab. It's it truly is just a
blend of all of the skills you've
learned on your way up to becoming a
leader and all of the things you've
learned about I really liked when my
leader did this or I really didn't like
when my leader did that. You have to
blend all of your experiences on the
technical side and on the personal side,
mix them together, and just become the
best leader that you can possibly be.
>> Yeah, I really like your point about
maximizing the output of your team. And
when I it was maybe my first or second
week at ZS and and she was a partner and
she said to me, Mike, what you'll
realize very soon is that your success
at your level at the time I was
consultant and it only gets this only
becomes more true uh the higher up you
go is your success
is only a fraction of what you do, Mike.
Your success is the people beneath you
and who support you. So, I think that's
true, especially as you get higher.
Like, think about a CEO, a CFO.
From a day-to-day basis, are they are
they the ones actually adding value to
the business? No. It's the people on the
manufacturing sites, it's the people
moving the product, it's the people
calling the vendors, it's the people
calling the retail outlets. So, to me,
what I realized was, well, unless I have
the support system around me, no matter
what, I can be the heart. I could give
200%. But if my if my ecosystem of
people around me are not giving me their
maximum output, I will fail unless I
somehow figure out how to get a 100
hours in the day and work 100 hours in
the day. So I think a key point here is
that leadership is about inspiring those
around you. Again, it's cliche, but how
do you get people out of bed in the
morning where they're coming into work
saying, "Yeah, my badge says yes, but
I'm excited to work for Mike or I'm
excited to work with Mike." And I think
maximizing that experience, I mean,
let's be real. The manager you said that
you loved, I've had great managers. Why
did we like them so much? Because it was
a great experience. And it's the same
thing with our clients. Why do they like
coming back to ZS? Is it because we're
we're giving them rocket science and
we're splitting the atom? Absolutely
not. It's because of the experience.
They like, yeah, our solutions work and
they're good for their business, but at
the end of the day, it's our leadership
and their experience with us that keeps
them coming back. So, that's just a
really long- winded way of me saying as
a leader, you need to make sure that the
people that are supporting you come to
work and maximize their output, like
John said. And if you don't have that
appropriate leadership style, people are
going to come into work and not support
you. And that's when you the ground
starts to get a little quaky and you
start to look around and say, "Wait a
minute, things are starting to fall
apart." Um, so I I really like that that
mindset of not does how does John or how
does Mike maximize the output of like
myself, but how do I get my team to do
that? I think it's a very very good
point.
All right, so with that again I know
your your time is very precious and I
appreciate the the 30 some minutes you
gave us here. So um definitely want to
say thank you on behalf of me and the
class um and for those who helped
collaborate putting this together. Um,
if there's any particular questions,
your uh your name will be somewhere on a
PowerPoint slide or somewhere on this
recording so they can find you on
LinkedIn. But definitely want to again
say John, thank you for for all your
time today and definitely look forward
to staying in touch.
>> Thank you. It was a pleasure.
>> Excellent.

lectures.lecture_name: Interview with Luke Liddy, Site Director at Mars

to understand what the industry is

technical solution
is super important because otherwise you

those leadership competencies and and

that. I would say my perspective on it

yes, you need to have interpersonal

I mean, we're talking executive level.
They're going to have the technical
experience in the background, but
they're not the subject matter expert in
the room most of the time. I'll give you
an example. When we do work here at ZS
and we a client comes to us and says,
"Hey, I have a problem and I want to

know the right people to bring in. They
know the tech expert. They know the

that's where that human piece comes in.
Identifying the expertise, having them
rally around you and power your engine.
So, I definitely 100% agree with you. It
depends on where you are in your career.
The the one question I do have as a
follow-up is so important is you said
you were currently working through, you
know, maybe some uh opportunities within
how you lead and identifying how you as
a person lead, right? Because we all are
different people. We have our
tendencies. I'm always doing that,
right? Crafting my leadership style. How
do you see the most effective way or
where do you get feedback um in your
organization on how you Luke lead your
team?
Is it like a formal process where you
have
>> I think me personally.
>> Are you there?
>> Yes.
>> Hello. Good.
>> Good.
>> Okay. Okay. Because there's like a weird
delay. Go ahead. Continue. Sorry.
>> You're good. You're good.
I can hear you. I just don't know how
how big of a delay there is.
Okay. So for me I guess in Mars we have
both informal processes and formal
processes. So we have definitely a
formal process for our leadership uh
like leadership to pipeline and like
higher level leadership. We have mentor
uh we have mentors, we have coaches, we
have you know certain levels of support
that the business provides um to give
you that feedback and kind of work
through some of those development
opportunities. But then there's also
informal feedback that you yourself can
glean from looking at some of those
other leaders and looking at people that
are in the positions that you eventually

modules.name: Quantum Literature and Business Implications
modules.description: This quantum technologies series course aims to give students a competitive advantage in the industry or academia by introducing them to Quantum technology. This is a niche but diverse field with various applications such as medicine, ML, Information Systems, and business. This course covers the foundations of this field and touches upon the different sub-topics. This is not a quantum physics/mechanics course or a mathematics course, and it will not cover the science or algorithms in depth. Still, it will discuss it enough to have a general understanding of the field from a business/computer science perspective. This course is intended to serve as an introductory course for Computer Science, Information Systems, Engineering, Accounting Information Systems, and Medical Information Systems. This is also a supplemental course for physics students interested in getting a business perspective on Quantum.

modules.name: Transformational Supply Chain Leadership 4: Know Your Craft, Leverage Your People
modules.description: As the world, and especially supply chains become more digital and automated, the connectivity across people becomes of elevated importance. Supply Chain leaders who want to transform their organization, best practices, and business goals must remain “human” in the design and execution of their supply chain. Building and bolstering relationships founded on trust is a core quality all transformational leaders in supply chain deploy

lectures.lecture_name: Interview with Sarah Harse, Senior Director at Johnson & Johnson

transcript.text:
All right, Sarah, it's so nice to see
you and thank you so much for agreeing
to speak with our class today. I wanted
to see if you could just um introduce
yourself and uh tell us a little bit
about what you do.
>> Sure. Um so my name is Sarah Harsh. I am
a senior director at Johnson and
Johnson. Um I've been with J&J about 10
years now. um which really started with
graduating from Ruckers with my MBA and
finding um that uh that next step into
the big corporate world um which is the
jump I was looking to make. I've spent
all my time at J&J within the
procurement function um and made a bunch
of jumps on that journey. But today I'm
a senior director in procurement who
manages all of the HR spend across the
organization which is a little bit north
of $2 billion. Um, and I get to manage a
global team. Um, and it's been a ton of
fun and I I can't wait to talk to you
guys more about leadership today.
>> Fantastic. Thank you. So, that's a nice
segue to my first question. Sarah, what
is a leadership skill or quality that
differentiates one leader from the rest?
>> You know, when I when I think about this
question, it brings me back to very
early days, probably when I was younger
than all of you. Um, I remember at 17 I
got asked to give a speech um around
leadership at like the National Honor
Society induction and like many people
do when I was asked to give a speech I
went and found a quote that was like the
inspiration for that speech. Um, and it
was all about how a good leader inspires
other people in their teams to do more,
achieve more, dream bigger. And at the
time it sounded like a great way to
craft a speech, but it's definitely
something that stuck with me as I've
grown in my career and taken on
positions of more and more kind of
leadership along the journey. Um, I do
think what really differentiates one
leader from another is how they empower
their team to be the star. You know,
it's your team that starts to get the
work done. The more senior you get in an
organization, the bigger your leadership
role, the less you do to make an outcome
happen. It's about the team. So, you
need to bring them on the journey. You
need to get them excited about it. You
need to give them the tools and skills
they need to be successful and you need
to make sure you're really amplifying
the success they're having. And that's a
big part about, you know, you being a
successful leader. You no longer deliver
the results. You really empower others
to do that and make sure that you know
they're really excited about that
journey. So, I think that's the big one
that I come back to that I think
everybody can do that a little bit
differently. how you approach it can be
very different and you can have a very
different style, but you're successful
as a leader if you get the people, you
know, around you to be successful and
that's a big part of it.
>> I I love that. I love that a lot. Um, my
next question is how do you make sure
that you're leading business in a
transformative or disruptive manner?
>> Yeah, I think it's an interesting time
to be in business. Um, obviously change
has been a constant for many many years,
but it just feels like the pace of that
change. It just is an exponential curve.
You know, it just it gets steeper and
steeper and that can be really hard. You
know, it can be really hard on us as
individuals. It can be hard on your
team. It's hard to kind of keep up with
the pace of change and not get
overwhelmed. Um, and so I think a lot
of, you know, really leading through
that is making sure your team is heard
is as you kind of move through that
journey. I think it's very challenging
to be someone in an organization and
just get, you know, kind of told this is
the new change, this is the new jump.
But if you don't understand why and you
didn't get a chance to shape some of how
we're going to approach that challenge
and give your ideas, you're not going to
be that bought in. And frankly, your
strategy as a leader is not going to be
great if you haven't heard from a broad
swath of the organization with a diverse
set of perspectives, a lot of different
kind of ideas and you know kind of
experience that they bring to the table
will help you make a more robust plan.
So I think leading through change is a
lot about how you bring people on the
change journey, how you bring their
ideas into the solution so that it feels
like everybody's really bought in and it
leads to that more robust solution at
the end of the day.
You're so right, Sarah. You're really
right. So, you kind of alluded to this
in your answer, but how do you rally
people around you?
You know, I think a lot of getting
people engaged and interested is really
around that one-on-one connection with
them, understanding where they're at,
kind of meeting them where they are, and
kind of taking those incremental next
steps that make sense for them. Everyone
is very different. Everyone has
different learning styles, different
kind of working styles. If you're not
transparent around kind of what you
bring to the table, what your, you know,
kind of tendencies are, and you're not
listening to that other person around
what they need and what they want,
you're going to find friction in that
inevitably because we're all so
different and we all have kind of
different perspectives. If you kind of,
you know, approach it from my style is
the way we're doing it and, you know,
I'm gonna manage in the same way for
every person, you're gonna find that
some people are going to do really well
because that's going to work for them
and some people are not. And you have to
kind of look at yourself and say, well,
what did I do that didn't kind of help
them along the journey? And so I think
it's really meeting people where they
are, kind of customizing your message
and approach to each of those folks that
really makes a difference and makes them
feel heard and excited and kind of
seeing that incremental progress is
super important. And if it's not
meaningful and personal, it's not going
to hit home for for individuals and
they're not going to want to go that
extra mile. So I think that's a big part
of becoming a people leader is like I
said up front, you stop doing a lot of
the work. it goes that the work is going
somewhere else and you spend way more of
your time on really helping the people
get the work done and helping them be
successful and figuring out what they
need from you to really, you know, help
them on that growth journey.
>> Great. That was so beautifully said. My
next question is, what do you think the
balance is between technical leadership
skills and human leadership skills? And
I could go into more detail or if you
need it or if you're okay with with that
question so far.
>> Yeah. The way I take that is it's kind
of a continuum.
You know, as you move through your
career, when you walk in the door at an
organization at say a more junior level,
entry level or, you know, early stages,
you need a lot of technical skills. You
know, you are going to be doing a lot of
that work. You're going to be living in
Excel and Tableau and all of these
things that are going to make you
successful. you have to do, you have to
know how to do that, you have to know
the ins and outs of, you know, the
actual technical skills that go along
with being successful. Um, but slowly as
you move up in the organization and you
maybe acquire your first people
leadership role, maybe you have two or
three people on your team, that moves
and you're going to spend maybe 50% of
your time doing the technical work and
teaching people how to do that technical
work and then 50% of your time figuring
out what your team needs and kind of
working on those soft skills. Um, and as
your team gets bigger and bigger, the
technical work you do shrinks. Um, and
that's not to say it's not super
important to understand it, to stay
fresh, but ultimately you really want to
keep building a team out underneath you
that has the new, latest, greatest
technical skills to bring to the table.
And you need to acknowledge that you're
not always going to be that expert
anymore. And that's a tough transition
when you're, you know, really taking
that first jump into people leadership.
It's really hard to shift from
individual contributor where I am
measured on what I deliver to this new
model of you're measured on what the
team delivers and your success is really
enabled by their success. And so you'll
see kind of that that continuum shift
and you'll need more of the soft skills
the further along you get. Um but it,
you know, really goes a long way to
continue to learn from your team. Know
that they have a lot of that skill. um
and make sure that you're kind of
staying in touch with what is the new
and latest and greatest thing. Not that
you need to know how to do it, but you
need to be able to really help your team
be successful at doing it.
>> That's a brilliant insight. Thank you so
much. Um my next question is, are there
certain skills or qualities you look for
in early graduates?
You know, I think there's a there's a
laundry list of things and I'm sure
you'll get a lot of great answers from
different people uh throughout your your
semester, but I think what stands out to
me is absolutely being able to do the
data analytics side of the world. You
know, that is entry point into um into
new roles and being able to harness all
this new technology that is coming at us
from every angle to make us faster, more
efficient. All of those things really um
differentiate early in career talent
because old people like me are slower to
figure out how to do all that stuff. You
know, I'm I'm learning how to use Gen AI
and ask the right questions, but I'm not
as good at it as some other people. So,
really being able to have that that
strong technical skill set is super
important. But I think what
differentiates folks that that gets you
in the door, you have to have it on your
resume is how you take the insights you
build and the kind of information you're
able to gather and articulate it and
communicate it to people. So much of
business is winning people over to your
perspective and influencing them to get
on board with a project. being able to
share things that matter to them and
facts and data that bring them along
your journey so that they'll support you
and and kind of take it to the next
level. And so I think it's, you know,
there's a technical skill set there, but
then I think where people really start
to differentiate themselves early in
career is how they figure out the
communication skill set that has to go
with it. Um, the other thing I would say
is just super critical right now with
this pace of change is learning agility.
You're never going to stop learning.
You're not going to graduate and then
never have to learn something again.
It's constant and you have to be
interested. You have to ask good
questions. If you think you're done, you
know, then you're probably going to get
stale very quickly. Um, and so it's
super important to stay curious and to
continue that education journey in all
the ways you can find, whether it's
through your, you know, kind of
corporate path and the education
opportunities they provide, experiential
learning, going back to school, all
those things are things you're going to
want to consider on this journey so that
you can stay up to date and bring, you
know, that latest and greatest to the
organization you worked for.
>> That's great. Wonderful. And this is my
last but not least question. Are there
skills you believe that all leaders must
have in supply chain?
>> A lot of what I talked about about
leadership throughout uh the last few
minutes I think applies everywhere. It's
not just a supply chain conversation. I
think what leaders in supply chain need
specifically above and beyond is the
ability to connect the dots and think
very end to end. You know, I it's funny
when I started in my career, my my first
or my supply chain career, I should say.
I started at J&J and I got a procurement
internship and I was walking in and
saying, you know, well, I I want to do
supply chain. And they were like, oh,
well, supply chain's over there,
procurement's over here. It, you know,
it was like the way the organization was
structured, you weren't speaking the
right language. And I had to figure out
how to weave together an end toend story
as to how all the work gets done, who
does the different pieces of it, how a
big complex organization functions, and
oftentimes take off the hat of I do this
function or I do this role and think
bigger because the way everything
interconnects just gets more and more
complex. So supply chain touches all the
way into marketing, touches all the way
back into kind of product development
and R&D. So you really have to be able
to connect across that broad spectrum.
Be really curious and interested and
learn about the kind of the full
ecosystem of the product you're
supporting because supply chain is going
to kind of interweave and be most
effective if you have a full
understanding. So it's I would say the
thing about supply chain is you're not
just supply chain. have to kind of be
everything and really slowly build an
understanding about the full picture
because where you find the efficiencies
may be in the connection points with all
those other groups.
>> That was really helpful and um you know
that concludes our conversation today.
Sarah, I really appreciate your time. I
know you're very busy and um we really
appreciate um your input, your insights,
and your advice today to our students
that are listening and watching today.
So, thank you so much.
>> Thank you for having me.

modules.name: Transformational Supply Chain Leadership 3: Execute Today, Eyes on Tomorrow
modules.description: As the world, and especially supply chains become more digital and automated, the connectivity across people becomes of elevated importance. Supply Chain leaders who want to transform their organization, best practices, and business goals must remain “human” in the design and execution of their supply chain. Building and bolstering relationships founded on trust is a core quality all transformational leaders in supply chain deploy
modules.matched_lecture_name: Transformational Supply Chain Leadership 3: Execute Today, Eyes on Tomorrow

modules.name: Transformational Supply Chain Leadership 2: Establishing and Optimizing a Value Chain Mindset
modules.description: As the world, and especially supply chains become more digital and automated, the connectivity across people becomes of elevated importance. Supply Chain leaders who want to transform their organization, best practices, and business goals must remain “human” in the design and execution of their supply chain. Building and bolstering relationships founded on trust is a core quality all transformational leaders in supply chain deploy

modules.name: Transformational Supply Chain Leadership 1: Managing a Supply Chain Ecosystem & Connecting the Dots
modules.description: As the world, and especially supply chains become more digital and automated, the connectivity across people becomes of elevated importance. Supply Chain leaders who want to transform their organization, best practices, and business goals must remain “human” in the design and execution of their supply chain. Building and bolstering relationships founded on trust is a core quality all transformational leaders in supply chain deploy

modules.name: Virtual Interaction and Virtual Work
modules.description: This module will present information about all aspects of technology at work. Included are discussions of the following questions: Why are online meetings so different from in-person ones? How do language and decision-making change when we move online, and when we move between a laptop and a cell phone? Exactly how costly is it to multitask, or to get distracted (even momentarily) by a message notification? What do your colleagues think about your tech-use choices?

modules.name: Stock Analysis using excel and AI
modules.description: This module is designed for students interested in learning about technical analysis applied in the financial markets by utilizing Excel and incorporating Claude, the RBS GenAI tool. The goal is to analyze, review and interpret a pre-defined trading strategy to a set of historical stock prices. Analysis will include returns, performance measures, and risk indications on a predefined set of historical market data. The approach will be refined into a strategy to analyze and review. You will use Excel and Claude to create a trading strategy, while developing and improving investment decision-making skills.
modules.matched_lecture_name: Stock Analysis using excel and AI

modules.name: AI Ethics in Business
modules.description: This module covers an Intermediate knowledge of AI technology and its current application in the business field. It also covers foundational topics of ethics and regulatory frameworks relevant to AI issues. The module also provides real-world case studies and future projections. This module integrates AI technology with business applications and social ethics, providing a unique and comprehensive learning experience. Participants will gain practical skills for implementing AI solutions responsibly and in compliance with regulations. The module connects theoretical concepts to actual business and ethical scenarios through case studies and real-world examples. Discussions on future AI trends and ethical dilemmas further prepare learners to lead in their fields.

modules.name: AI and ESG II: Implementing AI in ESG
modules.description: This course explores how AI—especially Generative AI—is transforming Environmental, Social, and Governance (ESG) practices by addressing challenges in data collection, analysis, and transparency. Students will examine AI applications in risk assessment, sustainable product design, resource optimization, and supply chain management, while also considering ethical issues such as data privacy, algorithmic bias, and explainability. The course further discusses the evolving regulatory landscape and highlights how AI can drive compliance, accountability, and long-term sustainable value creation.

modules.name: AI and ESG I: Foundations of AI and ESG
modules.description: This course explores how AI—especially Generative AI—is transforming Environmental, Social, and Governance (ESG) practices by addressing challenges in data collection, analysis, and transparency. Students will examine AI applications in risk assessment, sustainable product design, resource optimization, and supply chain management, while also considering ethical issues such as data privacy, algorithmic bias, and explainability. The course further discusses the evolving regulatory landscape and highlights how AI can drive compliance, accountability, and long-term sustainable value creation.

modules.name: Behavioral Finance
modules.description: This module explores how psychological factors and biases influence financial decision-making. It examines investor behavior, market anomalies, and the impact of emotions on risk and return.

modules.name: Retirement Planning
modules.description: This module introduces strategies for planning a secure retirement. It covers savings, investment options, social security, and employer-sponsored plans to build long-term financial stability.

modules.name: Risk Management Using Insurance
modules.description: This module explains how insurance is used as a tool for managing financial risks. It analyzes different types of insurance policies and their role in personal and business risk protection.

modules.name: Consumer Credit: Advantages, Disadvantages, Sources, and Costs
modules.description: This module examines the role of consumer credit in personal finance. It highlights benefits and drawbacks, explores sources of credit, and analyzes the true costs of borrowing.

modules.name: Taxes in Your Financial Plan
modules.description: This module shows how taxation affects financial decisions. It introduces tax planning strategies to minimize liabilities and improve after-tax returns.

modules.name: Portfolio Analysis
modules.description: This module introduces tools and methods for evaluating investment portfolios. It covers diversification, risk-return trade-offs, and performance measurement.

modules.name: Risk Management
modules.description: This module provides an overview of risk identification, measurement, and mitigation techniques. It applies risk management frameworks to both personal finance and investment decisions.

modules.name: Alternative Investments
modules.description: This module explores investment opportunities beyond traditional stocks and bonds. It covers real estate, commodities, hedge funds, and private equity as diversification tools.

modules.name: Bonds
modules.description: This module examines the role of bonds in financial markets. It explains bond valuation, yields, risks, and strategies for fixed-income investing.

modules.name: Stocks
modules.description: This module introduces the fundamentals of equity investing. It covers stock valuation, market behavior, and strategies for building equity portfolios.

modules.name: Estate Planning
modules.description: This module explains how to plan the transfer of wealth effectively. It covers wills, trusts, taxation, and strategies to preserve assets for future generations.

modules.name: Leading and Teaming with AI: Thriving in Future of Work
modules.description: This module explores how AI augments human capabilities and reshapes collaboration, leadership, and organizational roles. Students learn strategies for effectively leading teams that include AI systems and for adapting to emerging workplace dynamics.

modules.name: Strategic use of AI in Business Management
modules.description: This module covers how organizations can leverage AI to improve decision-making, optimize processes, and gain competitive advantage. Students examine business applications and learn frameworks for integrating AI into strategic management.
modules.matched_lecture_name: Strategic use of AI in Business Management
